chapter_summary:
Composition inversion addresses the fundamental inverse problem of mineral physics: given seismologically observed velocities and density as a function of depth (typically from the PREM reference model), what is the mineral composition of the mantle at that depth? This is the complement of the forward problem solved by the Mie-Gruneisen equation of state, which computes elastic properties from a known mineral assemblage. The inverse problem is inherently non-unique because different combinations of temperature, composition (Mg#, Fe content), and mineral proportions can produce similar seismic velocities. Nevertheless, systematic grid search methods can identify the best-fitting composition and characterize the trade-offs between parameters.

The approach implemented in this codebase (CompositionInverter) performs a one-dimensional grid search over the magnesium number Mg# = Mg/(Mg+Fe), which is the primary compositional variable controlling density and velocity in the mantle. For a given pair of Mg-rich and Fe-rich endmember minerals (e.g., Mg-bridgmanite and Fe-bridgmanite for the lower mantle, or forsterite and fayalite for the upper mantle), the algorithm linearly interpolates all eleven SLB2011 thermoelastic parameters between the endmembers at each trial Mg# value. The interpolated mineral is then passed through the MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer to compute Vp, Vs, and density at the specified pressure and temperature. A normalized misfit function chi^2 = ((Vp_calc - Vp_PREM)/Vp_PREM)^2 + ((Vs_calc - Vs_PREM)/Vs_PREM)^2 + ((rho_calc - rho_PREM)/rho_PREM)^2 quantifies the discrepancy between the calculated and observed properties. The Mg# that minimizes this misfit is the best-fit composition.

Anelastic corrections play a critical role in composition inversion because seismic velocities observed at teleseismic frequencies (approximately 1 Hz) are lower than the purely elastic velocities computed from the equation of state. The Anderson and Given (1982) correction V_anelastic = V_elastic * (1 - cot(pi*alpha/2)/(2*Q)) reduces elastic velocities by 1-3% in the upper mantle (where Q is low, approximately 80-143) and by less than 0.5% in the lower mantle (where Q is approximately 312). Neglecting this correction systematically biases the inversion toward compositions that are too iron-rich (lower Mg#), because the algorithm compensates for the velocity overprediction by adding denser, slower Fe-bearing material. The AnelasticCorrectionHelper in this codebase implements this correction and could be integrated into the inversion workflow for improved accuracy.

The temperature-composition trade-off is the central challenge in composition inversion. Higher temperature reduces seismic velocities (through lower elastic moduli and lower density), and so does higher iron content (through higher density and modified elastic moduli). A seismic velocity anomaly can therefore be explained by either a hotter or more iron-rich composition. The sensitivity kernel dlnVs/dT (Ch17) quantifies the thermal effect, while the variation of Vs with Mg# quantifies the compositional effect. Breaking this degeneracy requires either independent constraints on temperature (from geotherms or phase boundary observations) or simultaneous inversion of multiple observables (Vp, Vs, and density) that respond differently to temperature and composition changes.
prerequisite_knowledge: Elastic moduli (K_S, G) and seismic velocities Vp and Vs from mineral parameters (Ch04)
Mie-Gruneisen equation of state solver: computing properties at arbitrary P-T from SLB2011 parameters (Ch06)
SLB2011 mineral database: the eleven parameters per endmember mineral and their physical meaning (Ch05, Ch07)
PREM reference model: radial profiles of Vp, Vs, density, and pressure as functions of depth (Ch15)
Anelasticity and Q: velocity corrections from elastic to anelastic values (Ch16)
Sensitivity kernels: partial derivatives of velocity with respect to temperature and composition (Ch17)
Elastic mixing models: Voigt-Reuss-Hill averaging for multi-phase aggregates (Ch10)
Inverse theory fundamentals: misfit functions, parameter search, non-uniqueness, and trade-offs

key_equations.name: Normalized Velocity-Density Misfit Function

variables.V_{p,\\text{calc}}: P-wave velocity calculated from the mineral EOS at trial composition [m/s]
variables.V_{s,\\text{calc}}: S-wave velocity calculated from the mineral EOS at trial composition [m/s]
variables.\\rho_{\\text{calc}}: Density calculated from the mineral EOS at trial composition [g/cm^3]
variables.V_{p,\\text{PREM}}: PREM reference P-wave velocity at the target depth [m/s]
variables.V_{s,\\text{PREM}}: PREM reference S-wave velocity at the target depth [m/s]
variables.\\rho_{\\text{PREM}}: PREM reference density at the target depth [g/cm^3]

key_equations.interpretation: The misfit is the sum of squared relative residuals for Vp, Vs, and density. Using relative rather than absolute residuals ensures that each observable contributes comparably despite their different magnitudes (velocities in km/s vs density in g/cm^3). A perfect match gives chi^2 = 0. This is directly implemented in CompositionInverter.ComputeMisfit().

key_equations.name: Linear Interpolation of Mineral Parameters

variables.X_i: Any of the 11 SLB2011 parameters (V_0, K_0, K', G_0, G', theta_0, gamma_0, q_0, eta_{S0}, n, M)
variables.X_i^{\\text{Mg}}: Parameter value for the Mg-rich endmember
variables.X_i^{\\text{Fe}}: Parameter value for the Fe-rich endmember

key_equations.interpretation: All thermoelastic parameters are linearly interpolated between Mg and Fe endmembers as a function of Mg#. This is a first-order approximation that works well for solid-solution series where properties vary smoothly with composition. Non-linear mixing effects (excess volume, excess modulus) are neglected but are typically small for Mg-Fe substitution. Implemented in CompositionInverter.GridSearch() for all 11 SLB2011 parameters.

key_equations.name: Anelastic Velocity Correction (Anderson and Given 1982)

variables.\\alpha: Frequency exponent of Q (approximately 0.27 for olivine)
variables.Q_S: Shear quality factor at the observation frequency [dimensionless]

key_equations.interpretation: Seismic velocities at teleseismic frequencies are reduced relative to elastic predictions because of viscoelastic relaxation. This correction must be applied to calculated elastic velocities before comparing with PREM observations. In the upper mantle where Q_S is approximately 80, the correction reduces Vs by about 2.5%; in the lower mantle (Q_S approximately 312) the reduction is about 0.6%. Neglecting this correction biases composition inversions toward excessive Fe content. Implemented in AnelasticCorrectionHelper.Apply().

key_equations.name: Grid Search Optimization

variables.\\text{Mg\\#}_{\\text{best}}: Best-fit magnesium number that minimizes the misfit
variables.\\chi^2: Misfit function evaluated at each trial Mg#

key_equations.interpretation: The optimization is performed by exhaustive grid search over Mg# in a specified range (default 0.80-1.00 with 20 steps). At each grid point, the full EOS calculation is performed and the misfit evaluated. The grid point with the minimum misfit is returned as the best estimate. The misfit profile over the entire grid reveals the shape of the objective function, indicating whether the minimum is sharp (well-constrained composition) or broad (poorly constrained, large trade-offs). Implemented in CompositionInverter.GridSearch().

physical_concepts.concept: The Inverse Problem in Mineral Physics
physical_concepts.definition: Given seismological observations (Vp, Vs, density as functions of depth), determine the mineral composition and temperature of the mantle that best reproduces those observations.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: The forward problem (mineral parameters to velocities) is well-posed and deterministic. The inverse problem is fundamentally different: multiple combinations of temperature, composition, and mineralogy can produce the same seismic signature. This non-uniqueness means that additional constraints or assumptions are always required to obtain a meaningful solution.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: A common misconception is that finding a composition that matches PREM velocities proves that composition is correct. In reality, the solution is non-unique, and the best-fit Mg# depends strongly on the assumed temperature, the choice of endmember minerals, and whether anelastic corrections are applied.

physical_concepts.concept: Mg# as the Primary Compositional Variable
physical_concepts.definition: The magnesium number Mg# = Mg/(Mg+Fe) is the molar ratio of magnesium to total divalent cations, ranging from 0 (pure Fe-endmember) to 1 (pure Mg-endmember).
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Iron is heavier than magnesium (55.85 vs 24.31 g/mol), so increasing Fe content (lower Mg#) increases density. Iron substitution also reduces bulk and shear moduli in most mantle minerals, which lowers seismic velocities. The combined effect on velocity and density makes Mg# the most seismically observable compositional parameter in the mantle. For the Earth's mantle, Mg# is approximately 0.89-0.92 for pyrolite composition.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: It is sometimes assumed that Mg# affects only density. In fact, Fe substitution substantially modifies the elastic moduli (K and G) as well, and these modulus changes often dominate the velocity effect because velocity depends on the ratio of modulus to density.

physical_concepts.definition: The ambiguity arising because both higher temperature and higher iron content reduce seismic velocities, so a velocity anomaly can be explained by either thermal or compositional variations.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Consider a region with Vs 2% lower than PREM. This could be explained by a temperature excess of roughly 200-300 K at the same Mg# as the surrounding mantle, or by an Mg# decrease of roughly 0.02-0.03 at the same temperature, or by some combination. The trade-off can be partially resolved by using both Vp and Vs simultaneously, since the ratio R = dlnVs/dlnVp differs for thermal and compositional perturbations.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: Some assume the trade-off can be resolved by using density alone. While density is more sensitive to composition than temperature, seismological density constraints from normal modes have much larger uncertainties than velocity constraints, limiting their discriminating power in practice.

physical_concepts.concept: Role of Anelastic Corrections in Inversion
physical_concepts.definition: The reduction of elastic velocities to anelastic velocities that accounts for dissipative processes in the real Earth, which is essential for fair comparison between mineral physics calculations and seismic observations.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Mineral physics EOS calculations yield the elastic (high-frequency, unrelaxed) velocities. Seismic observations sample the anelastic (partially relaxed) velocities at lower frequencies. The difference is 1-3% for Vs in the upper mantle. If this correction is omitted, the calculated velocities are systematically too high, and the inversion compensates by adding more iron (which lowers velocity), producing a biased result with artificially low Mg#.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: A common error is to apply anelastic corrections only to Vs and not to Vp. While the Vs correction is larger, Vp is also affected through both the shear and bulk attenuation (QS and QK), as implemented in AnelasticCorrectionHelper.Apply().

physical_concepts.definition: The mathematical measure of discrepancy between calculated and observed properties, whose minimization defines the best-fit model.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Using squared relative residuals ensures each observable is weighted by its own magnitude, giving comparable sensitivity to Vp (approximately 10 km/s), Vs (approximately 6 km/s), and density (approximately 4-5 g/cm^3). Alternative misfit functions could weight Vs more heavily (since it is more sensitive to composition) or use absolute residuals, but relative residuals provide a natural normalization.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: Assuming that a small misfit value guarantees a unique solution is incorrect. If the misfit surface is flat (broad minimum), many compositions fit equally well, and the problem is poorly constrained at that depth.

key_references.author: Stixrude, L. and Lithgow-Bertelloni, C.
key_references.title: Thermodynamics of mantle minerals - II. Phase equilibria
key_references.why_essential: Provides the complete mineral parameter database (46 endmembers) and the thermodynamic formulation that underlies the forward model used in composition inversion. The interpolation between Mg and Fe endmembers in the grid search directly uses SLB2011 parameters.

key_references.author: Dziewonski, A.M. and Anderson, D.L.
key_references.title: Preliminary Reference Earth Model
key_references.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors, 25(4), 297-356
key_references.why_essential: Defines the PREM reference model that serves as the observational target for composition inversion. The misfit function compares calculated velocities and density against PREM at each depth.

key_references.author: Anderson, D.L. and Given, J.W.
key_references.title: Absorption band Q model for the Earth
key_references.why_essential: Establishes the anelastic velocity correction formula that bridges the gap between elastic EOS calculations and seismic observations. This correction is critical for unbiased composition inversion.

key_references.author: Jackson, I. and Faul, U.H.
key_references.title: Grainsize-sensitive viscoelastic relaxation in olivine: Towards a robust laboratory-based model for seismological application
key_references.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors, 183(1-2), 151-163
key_references.why_essential: Provides the experimentally calibrated Q model parameters needed to compute anelastic corrections as a function of temperature, pressure, frequency, and grain size.

key_references.author: Matas, J., Bass, J., Ricard, Y., Mattern, E., and Bukowinski, M.S.T.
key_references.title: On the bulk composition of the lower mantle: predictions and limitations from generalized inversion of radial seismic profiles
key_references.why_essential: Demonstrates systematic inversion of radial seismic profiles for lower mantle composition, explicitly addressing temperature-composition trade-offs and the importance of using multiple observables simultaneously.

key_references.author: Cammarano, F., Deuss, A., Goes, S., and Giardini, D.
key_references.title: One-dimensional physical reference models for the upper mantle and transition zone: Combining seismic and mineral physics constraints
key_references.why_essential: Pioneered the systematic comparison of mineral physics velocity predictions against 1D seismic models to constrain upper mantle composition and temperature, establishing methodology used in modern composition inversion.

worked_examples.problem_statement: Determine the best-fit Mg# for bridgmanite at 1000 km depth (P = 38 GPa, T = 2000 K) by grid search over Mg# from 0.80 to 1.00, comparing calculated velocities and density against PREM.
worked_examples.solution_approach: Use CompositionInverter.GridSearch() with Mg-bridgmanite (mpv) and Fe-bridgmanite (fpv) as endmembers. At each of 20 grid points in Mg# from 0.80 to 1.00, interpolate all SLB2011 parameters linearly, compute Vp, Vs, and density via MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer, and evaluate the misfit against PREM at 1000 km depth (Vp_PREM = 11340 m/s, Vs_PREM = 5885 m/s, rho_PREM = 4.405 g/cm^3).
worked_examples.numerical_values: PREM at 1000 km: Vp = 11340 m/s, Vs = 5885 m/s, rho = 4.405 g/cm^3, P = 34.56 GPa. The grid search evaluates 21 compositions from Mg# = 0.80 to Mg# = 1.00 in steps of 0.01. The misfit profile typically shows a minimum at Mg# around 0.90-0.95 for bridgmanite, consistent with the expected pyrolitic Mg# of approximately 0.89-0.92. The minimum misfit is on the order of 10^-3 to 10^-2, reflecting the fact that a single mineral cannot perfectly reproduce PREM (which represents a multi-phase assemblage).
worked_examples.interpretation: The best-fit Mg# falls in the expected range for lower mantle bridgmanite. The non-zero minimum misfit indicates that bridgmanite alone cannot match PREM; a realistic lower mantle also requires ferropericlase and Ca-perovskite. The U-shaped misfit profile around the minimum shows that composition is reasonably well constrained at this depth, with Mg# resolved to within approximately plus or minus 0.03.

worked_examples.problem_statement: Assess how neglecting anelastic corrections biases the best-fit Mg# for olivine in the upper mantle at 300 km depth (P = 10 GPa, T = 1500 K).
worked_examples.solution_approach: Run two inversions: (1) the standard elastic inversion comparing EOS-calculated velocities directly against PREM, and (2) an anelastically corrected inversion where calculated elastic velocities are first reduced using V_anelastic = V_elastic * (1 - cot(pi*0.27/2)/(2*Q_S)) with Q_S = 143 (PREM value at 300 km). Compare the best-fit Mg# from both inversions.
worked_examples.numerical_values: PREM at 300 km: Vp = 8648 m/s, Vs = 4672 m/s, rho = 3.466 g/cm^3, Q_S = 143. The anelastic Vs correction factor is 1 - cot(pi*0.27/2)/(2*143) = 1 - 1.097/(286) = 0.9962, reducing Vs by about 0.4%. For the upper mantle at shallower depths where Q_S is approximately 80, the correction is about 0.7%. The elastic-only inversion yields a best-fit Mg# that is lower (more Fe-rich) by approximately 0.01-0.02 compared to the anelastically corrected inversion. [uncertain]
worked_examples.interpretation: The bias from neglecting anelasticity is modest at 300 km depth (mid-upper mantle Q is moderate) but becomes more significant at depths of 80-220 km where Q drops to approximately 80 (the low-velocity zone). The systematic shift toward lower Mg# without anelastic correction demonstrates why proper treatment of attenuation is essential for reliable composition inversion.

worked_examples.problem_statement: Demonstrate the temperature-composition trade-off by computing misfit profiles at two different temperatures for bridgmanite at 1000 km depth.
worked_examples.solution_approach: Run GridSearch twice: once at T = 1800 K and once at T = 2200 K, both at P = 38 GPa and depth = 1000 km. Compare the best-fit Mg# and minimum misfit values.
worked_examples.numerical_values: At T = 1800 K: the lower temperature produces higher calculated velocities, so the misfit minimum shifts toward lower Mg# (more Fe needed to slow velocities down). At T = 2200 K: the higher temperature already reduces velocities, so the best-fit shifts toward higher Mg# (less Fe needed). The shift in best-fit Mg# between the two temperatures is approximately 0.02-0.04, corresponding to the temperature-composition trade-off of roughly delta(Mg#)/delta(T) = 0.5-1.0 per 100 K. [uncertain]
worked_examples.interpretation: This example quantifies the trade-off: a 400 K temperature uncertainty maps to an Mg# uncertainty of approximately 0.02-0.04. Since geotherms in the lower mantle are uncertain by roughly 200-400 K, the achievable resolution on Mg# from velocity inversion alone is limited to approximately plus or minus 0.02-0.03 without independent temperature constraints.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch06 Mie-Gruneisen Equation of State Solver
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: The forward model at each grid point in the composition inversion requires solving the Mie-Gruneisen EOS to compute Vp, Vs, and density from interpolated mineral parameters at the target P-T conditions.
The Mg and Fe endmember mineral parameters used in the grid search interpolation come from the SLB2011 database. The choice of endmember pair determines what mineral phase is being inverted.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch15 PREM and Radial Earth Models
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: PREM provides the observational target (Vp, Vs, density at each depth) against which calculated mineral properties are compared in the misfit function.
Anelastic corrections must be applied to elastic velocities before comparison with PREM observations. The Q values from Ch16 determine the magnitude of these corrections and their depth dependence.
Sensitivity kernels dlnVs/dT and dlnVs/dMg# quantify the temperature-composition trade-off that limits the resolution of composition inversion.

Real mantle rocks are multi-phase assemblages. Extending single-mineral inversion to multi-phase mixtures requires VRH or Hashin-Shtrikman averaging, where the volume fractions of each mineral become additional free parameters.
Predefined rock compositions (pyrolite, harzburgite, MORB) provide starting models for multi-phase composition inversion and define the expected mineralogy at each depth range.
The grid search approach in this chapter is the simplest inversion method. Bayesian MCMC methods (Ch29) generalize this to multi-dimensional parameter spaces with rigorous uncertainty quantification.

computational_methods:
The CompositionInverter implements a brute-force grid search algorithm over a single parameter (Mg#). The algorithm proceeds as follows: (1) Define the search range [Mg_min, Mg_max] and number of grid steps N (default 0.80-1.00 with N=20). (2) For each grid point i from 0 to N, compute Mg# = Mg_min + (Mg_max - Mg_min) * i / N. (3) Linearly interpolate all 11 SLB2011 mineral parameters between the Mg and Fe endmembers at this Mg#. (4) Pass the interpolated MineralParams to MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer to solve the EOS at the target (P,T) and obtain Vp, Vs, and density. (5) Look up PREM properties at the target depth via PREMModel.GetPropertiesAtDepth(). (6) Compute the normalized misfit chi^2. (7) Track the minimum misfit and corresponding Mg#. (8) Return an InversionResult containing the best Mg#, minimum misfit, and the complete misfit profile.

The computational cost is dominated by the N+1 EOS solutions, each of which involves an iterative pressure-matching loop (typically 5-15 iterations converging to 10^-5 GPa). For N=20, this requires approximately 20 EOS evaluations, completing in under 100 milliseconds on modern hardware. The grid can be refined with larger N for higher Mg# resolution, or a two-pass strategy can be used: coarse grid to find the approximate minimum, followed by a fine grid around it.

The current implementation does not include anelastic corrections in the misfit calculation; the ComputeMisfit method compares raw elastic velocities against PREM. To incorporate anelasticity, one would apply AnelasticCorrectionHelper.Apply() to the EOS output (Vp, Vs) using Q values from PREMModel.GetQSAtDepth() or from the parametric Q model before computing the misfit. This is a straightforward enhancement that would improve the physical fidelity of the inversion.
open_questions: How should non-linear mixing of elastic parameters between Mg and Fe endmembers (excess properties) be incorporated, especially for bridgmanite where ab initio calculations suggest non-ideal mixing?
What is the optimal weighting of Vp, Vs, and density in the misfit function, and should different observables be weighted differently at different depths based on their measurement uncertainties?
How does the iron spin crossover in ferropericlase (at approximately 40-90 GPa) affect composition inversion in the mid-lower mantle, where elastic softening may mimic compositional anomalies?
Can simultaneous inversion for temperature and composition be achieved with Vp and Vs alone, or is independent density information (from normal modes) essential to break the trade-off?
How should grain-size-dependent anelastic corrections be handled in inversion, given that mantle grain size is poorly constrained and varies with depth and tectonic setting?

What is the effect of aluminum and calcium substitution on inversion results for the lower mantle, where Al-bearing bridgmanite may have significantly different elastic properties than the pure Mg-Fe join?
exercise_suggestions: Run the CompositionInverter grid search for olivine (forsterite-fayalite) at 300 km depth (P=10 GPa, T=1500 K) with Mg# from 0.70 to 1.00 in 30 steps. Plot the misfit profile and identify the best-fit Mg#. Compare with the expected pyrolitic olivine composition (Mg# approximately 0.89).

Modify the misfit function to use only Vs and density (excluding Vp). Re-run the perovskite inversion at 1000 km. How does the best-fit Mg# change? What does this tell you about the information content of different observables?

Implement the anelastic correction in the ComputeMisfit method by applying AnelasticCorrectionHelper before comparing with PREM. Use PREM Q_S values from PREMModel.GetQSAtDepth(). Run inversions at 200 km, 500 km, and 1000 km depth and compare with the uncorrected results.

Demonstrate the temperature-composition trade-off quantitatively: run grid searches at T = 1800, 2000, and 2200 K for perovskite at 1000 km depth. Plot the three misfit profiles on the same graph and report how the best-fit Mg# shifts with temperature.

Extend the grid search to two dimensions: Mg# and temperature. Create a 2D misfit map over Mg# from 0.80 to 1.00 and T from 1500 to 2500 K at 1000 km depth. Identify the trade-off direction in (Mg#, T) space.

Compare inversion results for different mineral systems: perform grid searches for olivine at 300 km, wadsleyite at 450 km, and bridgmanite at 1000 km. Discuss how the misfit minimum sharpness (constraint on Mg#) varies with depth and mineral phase.

Investigate the effect of grid resolution on inversion accuracy: run the perovskite inversion at 1000 km with N = 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 grid steps. How does the best-fit Mg# converge? What is the minimum N needed for reliable results?

Apply the inversion to a hypothetical iron-enriched region by modifying the PREM target velocities by -1% for Vs and +2% for density. What Mg# does the inversion recover? How does this compare with geodynamic estimates for LLSVPs?

figure_suggestions.description: Misfit profile (chi^2 vs Mg#) for bridgmanite at 1000 km depth (P=38 GPa, T=2000 K). X-axis: Mg# from 0.50 to 1.00. Y-axis: normalized misfit (log scale). Show the U-shaped curve with a clear minimum.
figure_suggestions.axis_labels: X: Mg# [Mg/(Mg+Fe)]; Y: Misfit chi^2 [dimensionless, log scale]
figure_suggestions.key_features: Mark the minimum misfit point with Mg# value. Indicate the PREM-pyrolite expected Mg# range (0.89-0.92) with a shaded band. Show how the misfit increases steeply for Mg# below 0.70 (too iron-rich) and more gradually above 0.95.

figure_suggestions.description: Comparison of calculated vs PREM velocity profiles as a function of depth for the best-fit Mg# at each depth. Three panels: Vp, Vs, and density vs depth from 200 to 2800 km.
figure_suggestions.axis_labels: X: Depth [km]; Y-left: Vp or Vs [m/s]; Y-right: Density [g/cm^3]
figure_suggestions.key_features: Overlay the PREM profiles (solid lines) with calculated best-fit profiles (dashed lines). Highlight depth ranges where single-mineral inversion performs well (lower mantle) vs poorly (transition zone, where multiple phases are required).

figure_suggestions.description: Temperature-composition trade-off diagram: best-fit Mg# vs assumed temperature at 1000 km depth, showing the negative correlation between T and Mg#.
figure_suggestions.key_features: Plot the trade-off curve from T=1500 to 2500 K. Add a shaded region showing the geotherm uncertainty (approximately 1900-2200 K) and the corresponding Mg# uncertainty band. Mark the pyrolite Mg# for reference.

figure_suggestions.description: Effect of anelastic corrections: misfit profiles with and without anelastic correction at 200 km depth (where Q is low and the correction is large).
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show two curves: elastic-only (dashed) and anelastically corrected (solid). The corrected curve should have its minimum shifted to higher Mg# (less Fe). Annotate the Mg# shift between the two minima to quantify the bias from neglecting anelasticity.

figure_suggestions.description: 2D misfit map in (Mg#, T) parameter space at 1000 km depth, showing the trade-off valley.
figure_suggestions.axis_labels: X: Mg# [0.80-1.00]; Y: Temperature [1500-2500 K]; Color: log10(chi^2)
figure_suggestions.key_features: The minimum misfit should form an elongated valley running from high-T/high-Mg# to low-T/low-Mg#, illustrating the temperature-composition trade-off. Mark the global minimum and contour lines at 2x and 5x the minimum misfit.

historical_context:
The idea of inverting seismic observations for mantle composition has its roots in the pioneering work of Birch (1952, 1961), who established the systematic relationship between density, seismic velocity, and mean atomic weight, providing the first quantitative framework for interpreting the composition of the Earth's deep interior from seismic data. Birch's law, relating compressional velocity linearly to density for materials of similar mean atomic weight, showed that the mantle is composed predominantly of Mg-Fe silicates.

The development of self-consistent thermodynamic databases for mantle minerals, beginning with the work of Ita and Stixrude (1992) and culminating in the comprehensive SLB2011 formulation, transformed composition inversion from a qualitative exercise into a quantitative one. These databases enabled the computation of density and elastic properties at any P-T condition, making it possible to compare mineral physics predictions directly with PREM and other seismic reference models.

Cammarano et al. (2005) demonstrated systematic inversion of the upper mantle velocity structure for composition and temperature, establishing the methodology of comparing mineral physics-based velocity profiles with 1D seismic models. Matas et al. (2007) extended this approach to the lower mantle, explicitly addressing the temperature-composition trade-off and showing that Si/Mg and Fe/Mg ratios could be partially resolved by using Vp, Vs, and density simultaneously.

The importance of anelastic corrections in composition inversion was highlighted by Karato (1993) and quantified in detail by Goes et al. (2000) and Cammarano et al. (2003), who showed that neglecting attenuation leads to systematic biases in recovered composition, particularly in the upper mantle where Q is low. The recognition that anelasticity must be self-consistently included in any meaningful velocity comparison between mineral physics and seismology represented a major advance in the field.

Current research extends the simple grid search approach to multi-dimensional Bayesian inversion using MCMC methods, incorporating uncertainties in both mineral physics parameters and seismological observations, and addressing the effects of spin crossover, aluminum content, and water on the inversion results.
